preamble.summary: A psalm of David lamenting the disappearance of the faithful and the dominance of flattering, deceitful speech. The psalmist cries out for God to act against those who use smooth lips and a double heart. At the center stands a divine oracle: the LORD himself speaks, promising to rise and protect the poor. The psalm then contrasts human words — false, silver-tongued, manipulative — with the words of the LORD, which are pure silver refined seven times.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm is structured around the opposition between two kinds of speech. Human speech in the first half is characterized by duplicity: smooth lips (sifatei chalaqot) and a double heart (lev va-lev, literally 'a heart and a heart'). The wicked speak with two hearts — one visible, one hidden. Against this verbal corruption, the psalm places the words of the LORD (imrot YHVH), which are pure (tehorot), refined like silver smelted seven times. The number seven signals complete purification: God's words contain no alloy, no hidden agenda, no double meaning. The psalm is essentially a meditation on the trustworthiness of language itself — and concludes that only divine speech can be trusted without reservation.
preamble.friction: Verse 7 in the Hebrew (v. 6 in some English translations) contains the divine oracle — 'Now I will arise, says the LORD' — which interrupts the psalm with God's own voice. This is unusual in the Psalter; most psalms address God or speak about God, but here God speaks directly within the poem. The shift is jarring and powerful. The placement of the oracle at the psalm's center suggests that divine speech is the hinge on which the entire structure turns. Some scholars see this as a prophetic oracle delivered in the temple liturgy, with a priest or prophet speaking God's words during worship.
preamble.connections: The 'pure words' metaphor (v. 7) connects to Proverbs 30:5 ('Every word of God is refined') and Psalm 119:140 ('Your word is very pure'). The imagery of silver refined seven times appears nowhere else in Scripture, making it a unique intensification. The lament over the disappearance of the faithful (chasid) echoes Micah 7:2 ('The faithful have perished from the earth'). The promise that the LORD will arise (aqum) uses the same verb as the ark procession formula in Numbers 10:35 ('Arise, O LORD').

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Sheminith, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. On the sheminith. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: sheminith means 'the eighth' and likely refers to a musical mode, tuning, or an eight-stringed instrument. The exact meaning is lost. Some suggest it indicates a lower octave or bass register, which would suit the somber tone of this lament.

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹשִׁ֣יעָה יְ֭הוָה כִּי־גָמַ֣ר חָסִ֑יד כִּי־פַ֥סּוּ אֱ֝מוּנִ֗ים מִבְּנֵ֥י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Help, LORD; for the godly man ceaseth; for the faithful fail from among the children of men.
verses.rendering:
Save us, LORD, for the faithful are gone!
The trustworthy have vanished
from among the children of humanity.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm erupts with an imperative: hoshi'ah YHVH ('save, LORD!'). The urgency is explained by what follows: ki gamar chasid ('for the faithful one is finished'). The verb gamar means 'to be complete, to come to an end' — the chasid, the one characterized by chesed (faithful love, covenant loyalty), has ceased to exist. The parallel line intensifies: ki passu emunim ('for the trustworthy have vanished'). The emunim are those who embody emunah — faithfulness, reliability, steadfastness. Both terms describe people whose character mirrors God's own covenant attributes. The psalm begins with the terrifying claim that such people are extinct. The social fabric woven from faithfulness and trust has unraveled completely.
translator_notes: chasid ('the faithful one') derives from chesed and describes a person who practices covenant loyalty. It is the human counterpart to God's own chesed. When the chasid disappears, the covenant community loses its essential character.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal one, devoted one, godly, pious, one who practices faithful love
key_terms.note: chasid is the adjective formed from chesed ('faithful love'). A chasid is a person shaped by chesed — someone who embodies covenant loyalty in their relationships. The disappearance of the chasid is not just a social problem but a covenantal crisis.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful ones, reliable, dependable, those characterized by emunah
key_terms.note: emunim derives from emunah ('faithfulness') and the root aman ('to be firm, reliable'). These are people whose word can be trusted, whose character is solid. Their disappearance means truth itself has become scarce.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֤וְא ׀ יְֽדַבְּר֣וּ אִ֣ישׁ אֶת־רֵעֵ֑הוּ שְׂפַ֥ת חֲ֝לָק֗וֹת בְּלֵ֣ב וָלֵ֣ב יְדַבֵּֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They speak vanity every one with his neighbour: with flattering lips and with a double heart do they speak.
verses.rendering:
They speak emptiness to one another,
with smooth lips and a double heart they speak.
verses.expanded_rendering: The content of social discourse has become shav ('emptiness, worthlessness, falsehood'). The word shav is the same term used in the third commandment ('You shall not carry the name of the LORD for emptiness') — it describes speech that has been drained of substance and filled with deception. The phrase sefat chalaqot ('smooth lips, flattering lips') describes language polished to manipulate. The root chalaq means 'to be smooth, slippery' — these words glide in easily but have no integrity beneath their surface. The final phrase is devastating in its compactness: be-lev va-lev yedabberu ('with a heart and a heart they speak'). They have two hearts — one they show, one they hide. This is the anatomy of duplicity: speech that comes from a divided interior.
translator_notes: The phrase lev va-lev ('a heart and a heart') is one of the most concise descriptions of hypocrisy in the Hebrew Bible. It does not mean indecision (as in James 1:8, 'double-minded') but active duplicity — maintaining two simultaneous intentions, one public and one private.

verses.text_hebrew: יַכְרֵ֣ת יְ֭הוָה כׇּל־שִׂפְתֵ֣י חֲלָק֑וֹת לָ֝שׁ֗וֹן מְדַבֶּ֥רֶת גְּדֹלֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall cut off all flattering lips, and the tongue that speaketh proud things:
verses.rendering:
May the LORD cut off all smooth lips,
the tongue that speaks boastfully —
translator_notes: The verb yakhret ('may he cut off') carries covenantal overtones — karat is the verb used for both making and breaking covenants. To be 'cut off' is to be severed from the community and from God's protection. The smooth lips and boastful tongue will be surgically removed from the body politic.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשֶׁ֤ר אָמְר֨וּ ׀ לִלְשֹׁנֵ֣נוּ נַ֭גְבִּיר שְׂפָתֵ֣ינוּ אִתָּ֑נוּ מִ֖י אָד֣וֹן לָֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Who have said, With our tongue will we prevail; our lips are our own: who is lord over us?
verses.rendering:
those who say, 'With our tongue we will prevail!
Our lips are with us —
who is master over us?'
translator_notes: The boast mi adon lanu ('who is lord over us?') echoes the primal human impulse toward autonomy. It is not atheism — it is practical lordlessness, the refusal to acknowledge any authority that constrains speech or action.

verses.text_hebrew: מִשֹּׁ֥ד עֲנִיִּים֮ מֵאֶנְקַ֢ת אֶבְי֫וֹנִ֥ים עַתָּ֣ה אָ֭קוּם יֹאמַ֣ר יְהוָ֑ה אָשִׁ֥ית בְּ֝יֵ֗שַׁע יָפִ֥יחַֽ לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the oppression of the poor, for the sighing of the needy, now will I arise, saith the LORD; I will set him in safety from him that puffeth at him.
verses.rendering:
'Because the poor are plundered,
because the needy groan,
now I will arise,' declares the LORD.
'I will place him in the safety he longs for.'
translator_notes: This is one of the rare places in the Psalter where God speaks directly in the first person within a psalm. The oracle format — 'says the LORD' (yomar YHVH) — is typically prophetic, suggesting this psalm may have been performed with a priestly or prophetic voice delivering God's words in the liturgy.

The final phrase yapiyach lo is difficult. It may mean 'he pants for it' (the poor man longs for safety) or 'he [the oppressor] puffs at him' (the enemy sneers). We follow the reading that emphasizes the longing of the afflicted.

key_terms.semantic_range: to rise, stand up, get up, take action, establish
key_terms.note: qum in the mouth of God echoes Numbers 10:35 ('Arise, O LORD, and let your enemies be scattered'). When God rises, it is always to act — to judge, to save, to intervene. The word carries the full weight of divine agency entering a situation of human helplessness.

verses.text_hebrew: אִֽמֲר֣וֹת יְהוָה֮ אֲמָר֢וֹת טְהֹ֫ר֥וֹת כֶּ֣סֶף צָ֭רוּף בַּעֲלִ֣יל לָאָ֑רֶץ מְ֝זֻקָּ֗ק שִׁבְעָתָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: The words of the LORD are pure words: as silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times.
verses.rendering:
The words of the LORD are pure words,
like silver refined in a furnace on the ground,
purified seven times.
verses.expanded_rendering: After the divine oracle, the psalmist reflects on the nature of God's speech. The imrot YHVH ('words of the LORD') are tehorot ('pure, clean') — the same word used for ritual purity. God's words have no contamination, no alloy, no hidden agenda. The metaphor is metallurgical: like silver (kesef) that has been smelted in a crucible (ba-alil la-arets, 'in a furnace on the ground') and refined seven times (mezuqqaq shiv'atayim). Seven is the number of completeness — this silver has been purified beyond any possible impurity. The contrast with the smooth, deceitful human speech of verses 3-5 is total. Human words are empty (shav) and duplicitous (lev va-lev); God's words are pure silver with zero alloy. The psalm stakes everything on this distinction: human language lies, divine language holds.
translator_notes: The phrase ba-alil la-arets is notoriously difficult. alil may mean 'crucible' or 'furnace,' and la-arets may mean 'on the ground' (describing the location of the smelting operation) or may modify the refining process. The overall sense is clear even if the details are debated: God's words undergo the most thorough purification imaginable.

key_terms.semantic_range: sayings, utterances, promises, words (especially divine speech)
key_terms.note: imrah (singular of imrot) is used almost exclusively for divine speech in the Psalms. It differs from davar ('word, thing, matter') by emphasizing the spoken quality — these are God's actual utterances, his voice made verbal. When the psalmist says God's imrot are pure, he means every syllable God speaks is trustworthy.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּֽה־יְהוָ֥ה תִּשְׁמְרֵ֑ם תִּצְּרֶ֓נּוּ ׀ מִן־הַדּ֖וֹר ז֣וּ לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt keep them, O LORD, thou shalt preserve them from this generation for ever.
verses.rendering:
You, LORD, will guard them;
you will protect us from this generation forever.
translator_notes: The shift from 'them' (tishmerem) to 'us' (titserenu, though some read 'him') reflects the movement from the general promise to personal application. The 'generation' (dor) here means the current corrupt generation described in vv. 2-5 — the generation of smooth lips and double hearts.

verses.text_hebrew: סָבִ֗יב רְשָׁעִ֥ים יִתְהַלָּכ֑וּן כְּרֻ֥ם זֻ֝לּ֗וּת לִבְנֵ֥י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked walk on every side, when the vilest men are exalted.
verses.rendering:
The wicked prowl on every side
when worthlessness is exalted among the children of humanity.
translator_notes: The final verse is one of the most debated in the Psalter. ke-rum zullut could mean 'when vileness is exalted' or 'according to the exaltation of worthlessness.' The overall sense is that a society that elevates what is base creates conditions for the wicked to flourish. The psalm refuses to end with easy resolution — it acknowledges ongoing tension between God's promise and present reality.
